item.title: Yamaha BC3
item.description:
Photos Link to heading


Overview Link to heading

  • https://reverb.com/item/95025179-yamaha-bc3-breath-controller
  • https://www.tecontrol.se/products/usb-midi-breath-controller/breath-controller-facts
  • https://www.patchmanmusic.com/WindControllerFAQ.html - “What is a breath controller?”
  • https://yamahamusicians.com/forum/viewtopic.php?t=6851

Links to modern solutions & equivalents:

  • https://www.tecontrol.se/products
  • https://kelfar.net/bck-1-breath-controller/
  • https://www.sweetwater.com/store/detail/hb1MIDI--hornberg-research-hb1-midi-breath-station
  • https://midisolutions.com/prodbth.htm

Specification Link to heading

Basics

Features Description
Form Factor Mouth-controlled breath pressure sensor for Yamaha synthesizers
Manufactured 1982 - 2011

Manuals

item.title: Cordovox CL-10
item.description:
Photos Link to heading


Overview Link to heading

There isn’t a lot on the inT0Rw36z about Cordovox (nor do I expect there to be). Information that exists (unless I am missing it elsewhere) lives in Reverb & eBay sale listings or very old forum posts, so, I am thankful for the history below. It seems to be “comprehensive” even if it’s not actually “brief”.

item.title: Moog Liberation
item.description:
Photos Link to heading



Overview Link to heading

  • https://reverb.com/p/moog-liberation
  • https://www.vintagesynth.com/moog/liberation
  • https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Moog_Liberation

Specification Link to heading

Basics

Features Description
Architecture Analog / Subtractive (See below.)
Audio Mono output
Control Voltage Trig + Gate out
Form Factor 44-key synthesizer + performance controls
Manufacture Period 1980-81
Polyphony Monophonic dual VCO / Divide-down polyphonic OSC / Noise OSC

Manuals

item.title: Boss AW-3 Dynamic Wah
item.description:
Photos / Videos Link to heading




Overview Link to heading

  • Boss AW-3 on Reverb.com
  • Boss AW-3 Product Page

Specification Link to heading

Basics

item.title: Cheese
item.description:
It’s irrational how much I love cheese.

I know lots of other people do too, but it always feels slightly embarrassing to admit. I don’t love all kinds of cheese. I am particular about bleus & stinkers (I can’t really do super-stinky.) The ones I like, I like ok. They say Humboldt Fog is not a bleu cheese. It is to me.

The good news: there is an enormous range of cheese options to choose from without stank. Also, say what you will about Whole Foods, but they pretty much all have excellent cheese counters & people working behind them that have the 🧀 knowledge.

item.title: nonpunctual.org gear pages
item.description:
Overview Link to heading

I have some stuff.

Timeline Link to heading

Not like a hoarder. (I suppose this is relative…)

I don’t believe I have a problem (Doesn’t everyone who has a problem say that? Or, could it be I have way too much stuff? I’ll have you know I sold 8 items on Reverb.com !!)

I would say that if it’s a problem it started in earnest around 1984-85 when I got my 1st “real” synthesizer. My stuff now is way cooler, man. (Or, could it be that it’s only cool to me?)

item.title: nonpunctual.org website
item.description:
Overview Link to heading

I have long threatened to build a website. It’s a lot of work, even with help from my buddy Claude.

Timeline Link to heading

In 2002 I was getting pretty serious about computers after a few years of really only using them for music but I didn’t believe I could build my own website. So, I hired 2 different web designers for 2 ideas.

item.title: Roland Super JX MKS-70
item.description:
Photos Link to heading


Overview Link to heading

  • https://reverb.com/p/roland-mks-70
  • https://www.synthmania.com/mks-70_super_jx.htm
  • https://www.vintagesynth.com/roland/mks-70

Specification Link to heading

Basics

Features Description
Architecture 2 sections: (A & B), 2 DCO + 1 VCF + 1 VCA + 2 EG per voice
Audio combined mono output + (2) sets of stereo outputs (A & B), 1 for each section
Form Factor 19" rack mount module of the Roland Super JX JX-10 keyboard
Polyphony dual 6-voice or 12 voice modes

Manuals

item.title: No Free Space For Complexity
item.description:
It is amazing how systems can be connected today compared to 10-15 years ago.

Enterprise chat platforms were nascent, but ascendant then. Now, all sorts of fancy stuff probably happens in your chat app. Maybe you:

  • get notifications about computers & servers being configured
  • make tickets in a help desk system
  • do AI searches with bots in Salesforce data
  • get AI-generated sentiment summaries from customer calls

The sky is the limit! (Well, the sky, the amount of 💰 you have to spend on integrations, the complexity of your tools & the patience your IT person has to configure all of it…)

item.title: UTI: Uncomfortable In Any Context
item.description:
Info

These posts are being re-created from old Jamf Nation posts not because they are the best but because they may have useful things that were mangled in the Jamf Nation transition to its most recent hosting platform.

That, & they were always supposed to be blog posts anyway… I didn’t have a blog then.

Note

UTI in this domain unpacks to “Uniform Type Identifier” not “Urinary Tract Infection”. Get your mind out of the gutter…

item.title: The Endpoint
item.description:
What Is an Endpoint? Link to heading

The word “endpoint” is used a lot in tech. In the device management domain, an endpoint is any managed computer or mobile device that is actively used for work. This can mean servers, but it typically means devices that are deployed “1:1”, in end user’s hands. For most modern organizations 1:1 deployed endpoints are the primary portals to collaboration and productivity.

item.title: about
item.description:
Brock Walters is nonpunctual.


nonpunctual.org info:

  • link count = 249
  • page count = 36
  • word count = 29137

Glutton for punishment? Here’s more…

glossary Link to heading

Alphabetizer 🔡

Things must be alphabetized. (It sometimes feels like a sickness.)

I know the alphabet.

If I am trying to remember something, like someone’s name or a place I’ve been or a restaurant, I’m usually going through the alphabet & when I get to the letter, I remember it.

item.title: Lands End
item.description:
Description Link to heading

Lands End National Park is on the northwest edge of San Francisco. It seems like it should be a great area: dramatic views of the wide-open Pacific Ocean & the Golden Gate bridge, giant cargo ships from Asia at anchor, a little bit away from the heart of the city. It’s… ok.

The hike was nice, the company was great. Someone dumped a couch in a bushy area off one of the trails. Or, maybe they put it there to, like, sit on. (San Francisco is so weird.)

item.title: Yosemite
item.description:
Description Link to heading

Another one of California’s magical, beautiful places.

Photos Link to heading

Gorgeous summer day in Yosemite.

Thoughts Link to heading

This was a bizarre day trip. I will talk about it someday. For now, I’ll just enjoy the views.

item.title: Big Sur
item.description:
Description Link to heading

One of the most breath-taking, beautiful places on earth.

Photos Link to heading

A small set of pictures that doesn’t really do Big Sur justice…

Thoughts Link to heading

I was on a work trip for Fleet in SF & decided to take a few days to drive up & down the coast, going as far south as I could through Big Sur. I want to go back & spend a full week or 2 in the area. It looks & feels like a different planet to me.

item.title: Migration "Assistant"
item.description:
Info

These posts are being re-created from old Jamf Nation posts not because they are the best but because they may have useful things that were mangled in the Jamf Nation transition to its most recent hosting platform.

That, & they were always supposed to be blog posts anyway… I didn’t have a blog then.

Note

UPDATE 1: 20260305

When I wrote this script, it was intentionally for blocking Migration Assistant.app - I re-wrote it so any app can be blocked this way just by setting the variables at the top.

Note

UPDATE: Something I thought would never happen has happened. Since the beta features are under NDA, I guess I can’t really say what it is, but, IYKYK, &, it makes everything in this post mostly obsolete. :) Cheers.

item.title: Yule Logging
item.description:
Info

These posts are being re-created from old Jamf Nation posts not because they are the best but because they may have useful things that were mangled in the Jamf Nation transition to its most recent hosting platform.

That, & they were always supposed to be blog posts anyway… I didn’t have a blog then.


As it is the holiday season, I wanted to offer up a little gratitude in the form of 🎁 boilerplate code 🎁 related to topics that come up in the Mac admins #scripting & #bash channels again & again. I hope people find it here & find it useful.

item.title: Surfin' Safari
item.description:
Info

NOTE: These posts are being re-created from old Jamf Nation posts not because they are the best but because they may have useful things that were mangled in the Jamf Nation transition to its most recent hosting platform.

That, & they were always supposed to be blog posts anyway… I didn’t have a blog then.



It is an objective fact that The Beach Boys are one of the 5 most overrated musical acts of all time (the others are The Who, The Doors, The Grateful Dead, & Elvis Presley). However, that discussion will have to wait for another time & place…

item.title: Firefoxy
item.description:
Info

That, & they were always supposed to be blog posts anyway… I didn’t have a blog then.

Note

So,  added jq to macOS. I do have a funny story about this… The version I will tell here in public is: I was told that would never happen & then it did!

item.title: I've Got the Power
item.description:
Info

NOTE: These posts are being re-created from old Jamf Nation posts not because they are the best but because they may have useful things that were mangled in the Jamf Nation transition to its most recent hosting platform.

That, & they were always supposed to be blog posts anyway… I didn’t have a blog then.


An Extension Attribute script for Mac portable battery health:

#!/bin/bash
#shellcheck disable=SC2207

if ! /usr/sbin/sysctl -n hw.model | /usr/bin/grep -q -i 'book'
then
 echo "<result>no</result>"; exit
fi

IFS=$'\n'
pwrdata=($(/usr/libexec/PlistBuddy \
 -c "print 0:_items:0:sppower_battery_health_info:sppower_battery_health" \
 -c "print 0:_items:0:sppower_battery_health_info:sppower_battery_cycle_count" \
 -c "print 0:_items:0:sppower_battery_health_info:sppower_battery_health_maximum_capacity" \
 -c "print 0:_items:0:sppower_battery_charge_info:sppower_battery_max_capacity" \
 -c "print 0:_items:0:sppower_battery_model_info:sppower_battery_manufacturer" \
 -c "print 0:_items:0:sppower_battery_model_info:sppower_battery_firmware_version" \
 -c "print 0:_items:0:sppower_battery_model_info:sppower_battery_hardware_revision" \
 -c "print 0:_items:0:sppower_battery_model_info:sppower_battery_cell_revision" \
 /dev/stdin <<< "$(/usr/sbin/system_profiler -xml SPPowerDataType)" 2> /dev/null))

result(){
 echo "Health = ${pwrdata[0]}"
 echo "Cycle Count = ${pwrdata[1]}"
 
 if [[ "${pwrdata[2]}" == *'%'* ]]
 then
 echo "Max Capacity = ${pwrdata[2]}"
 else
 echo "Max Capacity = ${pwrdata[2]} mAh"
 fi

 if [ "$(/usr/sbin/sysctl -in hw.optional.arm64)" = 1 ] && /usr/sbin/sysctl -n machdep.cpu.brand_string | /usr/bin/grep -q 'Apple' && /usr/bin/uname -v | /usr/bin/grep -q 'ARM64'
 then
 i=5 # Apple Silicon
 else
 i=6 # Intel
 echo "Manufacturer = ${pwrdata[i-3]}"
 fi

 echo "Firmware Version = ${pwrdata[i-2]}"
 echo "Hardware Revision = ${pwrdata[i-1]}"
 echo "Cell Revision = ${pwrdata[i]}"
}

echo "<result>$(result)</result>"

Some notes:

item.title: Googalogically Speaking
item.description:
Info

NOTE: These posts are being re-created from old Jamf Nation posts not because they are the best but because they may have useful things that were mangled in the Jamf Nation transition to its most recent hosting platform.

Long version: I have seen lots of “curl the package then install the package” scripts. Some are ghastly, some are complicated (ahem, _APS…)

This is a (pretty dumb) script that will automate the execution of the “Mac Evaluation Utility” v. 4.0.1

item.title: Collect Year From Mac Marketing Model Name
item.description:
Info

NOTE: These posts are being re-created from old Jamf Nation posts not because they are the best but because they may have useful things that were mangled in the Jamf Nation transition to its most recent hosting platform.

That, & they were always supposed to be blog posts anyway… I didn’t have a blog then.


I’ve seen a few tricks for getting what Apple calls the Marketing Model name of a Mac in Terminal. One uses the last 4 characters of the serial number in a curl command. This seems to work (it requires the -L flag on curl). It may stop working because of changes to Apple serial numbers.

item.title: Removing System Roots (is probably a bad idea)
item.description:
Info

These posts are being re-created from old Jamf Nation posts not because they are the best but because they may have useful things that were mangled in the Jamf Nation transition to its most recent hosting platform.

That, & they were always supposed to be blog posts anyway… I didn’t have a blog then.

Note
I haven’t tested this recently. I am pretty sure you can’t do it. I guess you can test if it’s possible by disabling System Integrity Protction (SIP) but that might not even work. The script techniques are still vaguely interesting…

There may come a time when you need to delete a System Root certificate. This is not something you should do lightly, but, maybe a cert was installed by an update that you know is bad. Maybe a cert is expired. This isn’t a huge deal, but, there’s no reason for it to be there.

item.title: The date command is pretty hard to use...
item.description:
Info

These posts are being re-created from old Jamf Nation posts not because they are the best but because they may have useful things that were mangled in the Jamf Nation transition to its most recent hosting platform.

That, & they were always supposed to be blog posts anyway… I didn’t have a blog then.


So, I don’t know about you, but, when I use the date command it’s usually to match some arcane format. This means I am then scrolling to the bottom of the date man page to find the name of the man page where all the format options are listed. Every time is just like the 1st time, but, not in a good way! (It’s strftime by the way… good luck remembering it.)

item.title: Unify Your macOS Terminal Command History
item.description:
Info

These posts are being re-created from old Jamf Nation posts not because they are the best but because they may have useful things that were mangled in the Jamf Nation transition to its most recent hosting platform.

That, & they were always supposed to be blog posts anyway… I didn’t have a blog then.


I’m embarrassed to not have known this until now, but, the joy in the discovery outweighs the pain of embarrassment, so, here goes.

item.title: San Jose
item.description:
Description Link to heading

I can’t find the article, but I believe it was in a ‘New Times’ ranking of the least affordable cities in America that I saw a quote about San Jose I will never forget:

Photos Link to heading

Grungy walk around the edge of SJ.

item.title: Thinking About Extension Attributes
item.description:
Info

These posts are being re-created from old Jamf Nation posts not because they are the best but because they may have useful things that were mangled in the Jamf Nation transition to its most recent hosting platform.

That, & they were always supposed to be blog posts anyway… I didn’t have a blog then.

Note
For other Jamf Nation posts under my brock_walters “employee” account, see: https://community.jamf.com/search/activity/reactions?userid=45006

One great thing about JAMF Nation is that awesome workflows can be shared so easily. Sometimes, however, ones that are slightly less than optimal get propagated. The other day I happened to come across an instance of this that I thought maybe I could help explain.

item.title: contact
item.description:
Point the camera on your pocket computer at this QR code to send me a message.


(Or, if you've used a computer in the last, say, 35 years with some careful deduction you can probably figure out where to send it. I'll help: It's not just my 1st name & it's not just my last name. It's my "wholename".)
